frontmatter.title: 4. AI and language
frontmatter.subtitle: Writing, dialogue, and large language models

children.value: Of all the generative tools we will meet,
are the ones most students already use every day. They draft emails, summarise readings, fix code, explain concepts, brainstorm, translate, and
. (We will talk about that last one.)
This chapter is about what is actually happening when you type into ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, or a local model. It is also about how to
as a tool for writing and thinking — and how to spot when they are quietly making things up.
A language model is a system trained to
) given the previous words. That is it.
”, the model assigns a probability to every possible next token. The probability of “Oslo” should be high; the probability of “purple” should be low.
text, the model samples the next token, appends it to the input, and repeats:

The capital of Norway is ☐ → Oslo
The capital of Norway is Oslo ☐ → .
The capital of Norway is Oslo. ☐ → It
This is the same loop whether the model has 1 million parameters or 1 trillion. Modern LLMs are scaled-up versions of GPT-style models from 2018–2020
. Everything else — chatting, coding, reasoning, refusing to answer — is built on top of that single trick.
The model does not see words; it sees
. A token is usually a piece of a word — common words become one token, rare words become several. “Oslo” might be one token; “Jensenius” might be three. You can play with this in the
Most commercial models bill per token. Long prompts and long answers cost more.
Each model has a maximum number of tokens it can attend to at once (its
). Outside that window, information is invisible to it.
Non-English languages often tokenise less efficiently. Norwegian text typically uses more tokens than equivalent English, so it costs more and fits into less context.
between conversations unless a system is built around them to provide it. What they have is a
: a buffer holding the conversation so far (system instructions + user messages + model responses). Anything outside that buffer simply does not exist for the model.
This is why “remember that we are writing a fantasy novel” works inside a chat (it stays in context) but does not transfer to a new chat (the buffer is gone). Tools that
to remember (custom GPTs, ChatGPT memory) achieve this by quietly pasting relevant snippets back into the context.
was that you can teach an LLM new behaviour just by
showing it examples in the prompt

Translate to Norwegian.

EN: The library is open.
NO: Biblioteket er åpent.

EN: Where is the train station?
NO: ☐
The model uses the pattern in the prompt to fill in the next answer. This is why
is now a real skill: you are programming the model with examples, not with code.
— just ask. Works for common tasks the model has seen a lot of.
— give 2–6 examples of input/output before your real request.
before answering. Often improves reasoning, sometimes at the cost of length.
For non-trivial tasks, this skeleton works well:

ROLE: You are a [role with relevant expertise].

TASK: [What you want done, in one sentence.]

CONSTRAINTS:
- [Length, style, format]
- [What to avoid]
- [Audience]

CONTEXT:
[Any background the model needs.]

OUTPUT:
[The exact shape you want, with placeholders or an example.]
magic. It is the same template you would write for a freelancer.
The failure modes you need to recognise

children.label: The failure modes you need to recognise

children.value: LLMs fail in characteristic ways. You will see all of these by week 6.
The model generates a confident, fluent statement that is

. It might invent a paper title, a court case, a quote, or a study. This is not a bug to be patched — it is a direct consequence of the next-token training objective, which rewards plausibility over truth
and check them. (Beware: the model can also hallucinate sources.)
to the model’s strengths (e.g., rewriting, summarising provided text).
— paste the article and ask the model to answer from

The model agrees with you, even when you are wrong. Tell it that 2 + 2 = 5 and confidently insist, and it will often capitulate. This is a side effect of training procedures that reward “helpful” answers.
Ask “is the following correct?” rather than “I think X is correct, right?”
The model produces three paragraphs where one sentence is needed. Solution: ask for fewer words. Specify the exact format. Models obey length instructions reasonably well in 2026.
A long generation drifts in tone or style. Solution: regenerate from a fresh prompt every few hundred words.
LLMs are not calculators. They will confidently get 17 × 23 wrong. Modern chat products fix this by giving the model access to a code interpreter. If you are doing anything numeric,
make sure the model is running code
Models have a training cutoff. They do not know what happened yesterday unless they have web search. Always check the cutoff if recency matters.
You will work with two kinds of LLM this semester:
— OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral (some). You access them via a website or API. The weights are not public. They tend to be the most capable.
— Llama, Mistral (some), Gemma, Qwen, DeepSeek, etc. You can download the weights and run them yourself, on a laptop or a server. They lag the frontier by 6–18 months but are closing the gap.
research, reproducibility, sensitive data, or learning
, prefer an open-weight model you can run locally (e.g., via
, weigh privacy, cost, latency, and quality.
How to write with an LLM

children.label: How to write with an LLM

children.value: A working pattern that holds up across disciplines:
Make a bullet outline yourself. Do
ask the model to brainstorm from scratch — that path leads to bland, average prose.
Use the model to argue with the outline.
“What is missing? What is wrong? What audience would object?”
Write a rough version of each section.
“Make this paragraph half as long. Make this sentence clearer. Suggest three alternative openings.”
Keep them in a file alongside your draft.
You should treat the LLM as a
— useful, opinionated, sometimes wrong, never to be trusted on anything that matters without a check.
from your discipline (a paragraph, an explanation, a critique, a translation, a summary…).
For each, build a prompt using the
Run each prompt in two different LLMs (e.g., one commercial, one open-weight via Ollama). Save the outputs.
: where do the models differ? Which one made which mistakes?
on a niche topic in your field (something obscure enough that it might bluff).
Try to find each reference. How many actually exist? How many are partially real (real authors, wrong title; real title, wrong year)?
Write a short note (200 words) on what you found.
Python package to call a model from a notebook. Try:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You answer in one sentence."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "What is the etymology of the word 'fjord'?"},
    ],
)
print(resp.choices[0].message.content)
(Replace the API key and model name with whatever your institution gives you. UiO has its own

How would you tell, in five seconds, that a paragraph in front of you was written by an LLM? Test your heuristic on three short paragraphs (a mix of your own, a model’s, and a colleague’s) and see how often you are right.
European Parliament,Council of the European Union, 2024
introduces transparency requirements for “synthetic content”. What would meaningful labelling look like for an essay drafted with an LLM?
Where, in your own writing process, is the LLM most useful? Where is it actively in the way?
